reviews.ownerResponse: Hello this is attorney Donald Howard.  14-296aa(b)(1) is a moving violation in Connecticut. "Complete defense for non-moving violations starting as low as $199 (plus court cost, if any),"  is in bold and clearly written on the first page of the website. My firm's legal fees are not base on the fine. When you plead not guilty, there is no fine payment until you accept the state's offer or lose at trial. Even then, you have a period to appeal, and you still wouldn't have a fine due until you lose the appeal. The attorney fee you were quoted is a flat fee that covers our attorneys travel time to whatever court in the state of CT that your ticket will go to if a hearing is necessary. It covers the attorney's court time, so you have the convenience of going to work or school and not waiting with the hundreds of other people in traffic court. It covers Brandy and the paralegals that process your online ticket review, request the police report, DMV reports, body camera/ dash camera, calibrated laser or radar reports. It also covers the attorney's review of all that information and presenting a defense to the state's attorney on your behalf to possibly get your case dismissed. Lastly, it covers you being fully informed of the outcome of your case and prepared by our attorneys to defend your position in a trial if you elect to have one. If you do not think $495.00 is worth all of the value above, you could have asked for a referral to a more affordable law firm vs. leaving a one star review without hiring us. This is a malicious review and will be reported on all platforms that you have left this on.

I cannot recommend Eric Posmantier highly enough for his exceptional representation of my case. Throughout my divorce Eric provided both expert legal guidance as well as emotional support - both of which are needed to successfully get through a divorce. His clear explanation of court procedures, the actual practicalities of it, and documentation requirements, helped me understand what to expect at each stage. In my view what sets Eric apart is his genuine concern for clients as individuals with unique needs. He took the time to understand my personal circumstances and priorities before crafting our strategy, and always prioritized the human aspect of what can easily become a mechanical process. His clear communication, attention to detail, and strategic approach helped secure a settlement that protected my interests and rights, and always gave me options to consider. Eric's deep knowledge of family law combined with his patient, empathetic demeanor made a very difficult situation manageable. Going through divorce is 50% process and 50% emotion - and Eric covered 100% of it. If you're facing divorce proceedings, I would strongly recommend Eric Posmantier to help you through it.

He understood my abilities and my lack of abilities.  He fully read the opposing team and their antics/personalities/“wrong doings” to the max degree.  He was emotionally there for me…as I felt scared and weak.  Being a stay at home mom is a wonderful opportunity I was afforded in life.  But a divorce process is extremely intimidating to this inferior position.
When there is a sense of no control or ability to access family money-this feels like drowning.
Eric was a gentleman and moved with professional integrity while being challenged with extreme undue stress from the opposing team. If you feel like an underdog-you need Eric-I can only speak from that vantage point.
I am better than ever-it’s taken me time to move past the negative experience.  My financial situation and future are stable thanks to this team…they play fair and win.  I will always chose to move in life with integrity - exactly how Eric operates!

Beneficiaries Treated Like Defendants

If I could give zero stars I would.

My husband was named a beneficiary in his late uncle’s will. Unfortunately, due to some tricky family dynamics, the executor named was someone who was not impartial by any stretch of the imagination. I assume the uncle had hoped that the named executor would have been able to set her own animosity in effort to attend his final wishes. The executor (an estranged aunt) hired this firm to represent the estate to ensure the assets were distributed in accordance with the law and navigate all interactions that may arise. We were quite thankful that she did this initially, because otherwise we assumed the dysfunctional family dynamics would bleed into the estate management. We received communication that we were named beneficiaries, and then received an initial accounting. That was over a year ago.

At first, the attorney overseeing the case seemed quite friendly and willing to help us understand the process. We had never been through formal probate before and some of the assets were unique. After a couple of phone calls in which my husband tried to discuss what the process would be moving forward, the attorney began accusing him of being hostile with her. And the accusations were not even discussed directly with him, instead talking about their conversations *with the executor.* He still has no idea what she could’ve been talking about, except for that he was becoming frustrated trying to hear her as our children (2, 4 and 6) were quite rambunctious in the background on that particular day. On the flip side, my husband once called her, and she answered the phone with “What?!” At one point, she even mockingly laughed at him for asking what to expect moving forward because “the house hasn’t even sold yet.” I was taken aback by how unprofessional this would be for anyone, especially an attorney dealing with delicate family matters. She has never given us the opportunity to ask questions in detail, as she is often redirecting the questions or avoiding them altogether. The attorney handling our case has repeatedly made my husband feel ashamed for asking questions about the process and what to expect. It was our understanding that beneficiaries have a right to certain information – such as when we can expect things to wrap up, when we can expect certain documentation, what to expect in the process, or understanding what is still remaining in effort to close the account. We don’t even know where the distribution of assets will be coming from at this point – is that the executor? Or the law firm? We are consistently denied any real, concrete answers or treated as suspects in criminal proceedings.

The last we spoke with the attorney, she was not pleasant at all to deal with. This was in February, at which point she shuffled my husband off the phone with assurances we would hear from her firm in “a couple months.” It is June 2024, and the proceedings were supposed to have been completed in April (per the order of the court). We have received no official communication whatsoever since September 2023. We have left three voicemails since May to understand what we can expect in terms of a timeline and further documentation, yet the attorney has not called us back. The executor has deferred all communication to the attorney. As you can imagine, it’s hard to get questions answered when the executor refuses to answer them and the attorney ("neutral party") does not return phone calls. It grinds my gears that part of the inheritance that will benefit my children will be spent on this abysmal service.

As anyone who has ever been divorced or dealt with a challenging family dynamic can attest: you really don’t want to have a financial attachment dragging on unnecessarily as it causes an immense amount of distress. If you are looking for a professional firm that will handle your estate and family with decency and respect, please look elsewhere.

From day one, they showed not only legal expertise but genuine compassion. Alan took time—real time—to explain what each phase of the case would look like, including what costs could arise. Their transparency made a complicated process feel manageable. I always knew what was happening and why.

When the first settlement offer came in (and it was far too low), Alan and Tracy didn’t flinch. They helped me explore legal funding options so I could pay my bills and feed myself while we pushed for a result that truly reflected the damage done. In the end, we won a settlement that allowed me to replace my severely damaged vehicle, cover the repairs, and move forward.

But beyond the outcome, what really stayed with me was the respect Alan gave me—not just as a client, but as a woman and as an artist. He understood my path and treated me with care, not condescension. That matters deeply.

Both Alan and Tracy made me feel like more than just a case number. I only wish I had gone to them for earlier injuries—I’ll never go anywhere else now.

If you’ve been hurt and don’t know where to turn, turn here. You’ll be in the best possible hands.

I ended the session asking him what his issue was - he was short with me and seemingly annoyed / frustrated the entire time. He kept repeatedly asking me if I sent him the email I said I would, and I kept saying yes. His attitude was dripping with disdain and misogyny. His line of questioning was if he thought I was stupid. He actually began speaking really slowly, and very condescending- like, as if I didn’t understand what email was, or how to send an email?

I’ll never forget this extremely shocking and disappointing interaction with John Jowdy.
